items.q: What is the relationship between atman and Brahman in Hindu philosophy?
items.correct: Atman is the individual self/soul; Brahman is ultimate reality. In Advaita Vedanta, atman IS Brahman — the self is identical with ultimate reality, and liberation comes from realising this non-duality.
items.misconception: Atman is the body and Brahman is the soul.
items.random_wrong: Atman and Brahman are two competing Hindu gods.
items.why: The atman-Brahman identity is the central insight of Advaita. Students invert the soul/body labels or treat both as deities. The teaching is metaphysical: separation between self and ultimate reality is illusion (maya).

items.q: What is the Eightfold Path in Buddhism?
items.correct: The practical path to ending suffering: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration — grouped into wisdom, ethical conduct and mental discipline.
items.misconception: The Eightfold Path is the same as the Four Noble Truths.
items.random_wrong: The Eightfold Path is eight gods Buddhists must please.
items.why: The Four Noble Truths diagnose suffering; the Eightfold Path is the fourth truth — the prescription. They are connected but distinct. Students conflate them or treat the Path as a deity-list.

items.q: What do Christians mean by resurrection, and how does it differ from reincarnation?
items.correct: Resurrection is the once-for-all bodily raising to eternal life at the end of time (modelled on Christ's resurrection); reincarnation is the soul's repeated rebirth into new bodies. They are distinct doctrines from different religious traditions.
items.misconception: Resurrection and reincarnation are basically the same idea of life after death.
items.random_wrong: Christians believe everyone is reincarnated until they accept Jesus.
items.why: Christianity affirms one bodily resurrection, not cyclical rebirth. Students flatten different afterlife concepts into a single 'life after death' idea, missing the very different metaphysics involved.

items.q: Whose 1902 Gifford Lectures examined mystical experience?
items.why: It's William James. “Rudolf Otto” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which 16th-century Spanish mystic wrote Interior Castle?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Teresa of Avila. “John of the Cross” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Otto publish Das Heilige?
items.why: Exact answer: 1917 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
